data.title: DHT Holdings - An Interesting Value Play Despite Low Growth
data.description:
Introduction

DHT Holdings (NYSE: DHT) is a Bermuda company that operates a large fleet of oil tankers. This article will show why DHT Holdings could be an interesting value play, due to its strong position compared to its competitors, combined with a low valuation.

Fundamentals

DHT is an overall profitable company, with gross profit margins of 54.84%, 20% higher than the sector median, and an EBITDA margin of almost 50%. Furthermore, the company has, for the sector, a high ROE of 17.56% in contrast to a sector median of only 10.35%, indicating high quality and a supply of good investment opportunities.

This can also be seen in the ROE growth of 14.1%. Apart from that, however, the company has overall low growth, as can be expected in the oil shipping sector. While revenue growth of 2% is standard for the industry, EBITDA growth is even negative at -2.49%. Nevertheless, forward growth is expected to be a bit higher, between 6% and 8% for both measures. In terms of valuation, the company is quite cheap at an EV/EBITDA ratio of 7.37 and a P/E ratio of 9.74. Figure 1 shows DHT's P/E ratio over time. As can be seen, the stock was historically only cheaper at the end of 2020, when oil prices were still low, and in 2024.

However, in terms of the P/S and P/B ratios, DHT is still slightly elevated at numbers of 3.08 and 1.68, respectively.

An additional argument that can be made for DHT is its high dividend yield of 8.71% ($0.95 a share), which is in the higher historic ranges, as can be seen in Figure 2, showing the dividend per share over time. Nevertheless, the diagram also shows that the consistency of dividend payouts is relatively low, which is partly due to the high volatility of oil prices.

In terms of the recently released first quarter 2025 earnings results, the company entered into two new charters for one year at $92,500 a day. Meanwhile, TCE is down in comparison to Q1 2024 at only $38,200 a day in compared to $45,200. In terms of sales, DHT sold DHT Scandinavia at a $19.8 million gain, while paying $25.6 million in new building installments. In terms of utilization, operating and revenue days are down in comparison to Q1 2024 at 2,084.2 and 2,076.9 days, respectively. While the unscheduled off-hire of 0.0% indicates great utilization, it can also be seen as a sign that the company has too many employees and might yet have to put more investments into its fleet.

Concerning the sustainability of the dividend, I am at least not very concerned for the next year. The company currently has a strong balance sheet, shown by the low debt/equity ratio. Expenses are down year over year, and the dividend is covered by earnings and cash flow. While tariffs are a concern, I believe that DHT will continue paying its dividend as long as oil prices remain elevated. Nevertheless, I do not want to exclude the possibility of at least a lower future dividend.

Peer Group Analysis

In its peer group, DHT is one of the most profitable players. While companies like TORM plc. (NASDAQ: TRMD) and International Seaways, Inc. (NYSE: INSW) have slightly higher revenue and EBITDA margins; others like Global Partners LP (NYSE: GLP) and Genesis Energy (NYSE: GEL) are far below DHT, with margins of less than 25%.

In terms of growth, DHT is mixed but still belongs to the higher bracket of companies. While TORM and GLP have positive revenue growth rates of 2%-4%, other companies like Nordic American Tankers Limited (NYSE: NAT), INSW, and GEL are showing negative revenue growth between -6% and -12%. All of this shows that DHT belongs to the top players in its industry in terms of market position.

Figure 3 shows the valuation metrics of DHT and its competitors. Additionally, it shows Seeking Alpha's growth and profitability grades to get a feeling of how good the company is in other sectors (the idea is that a growing, more profitable company should have a higher valuation than one that is declining and unprofitable). As can be seen, DHT belongs to the more expensive bracket, having the highest EV/EBITDA and P/S ratios. The same can also be said for the P/E ratio, which is only lower than that of NAT and GLP. Nevertheless, the P/B ratio is comparably small, which should indicate a higher margin of safety for investors. Considering that DHT is the only company in the list that seems solid to good in both the growth and profitability categories, it would even be fine for it to be the most expensive company. However, at a valuation like this, the company seems cheap.

Other Factors

Apart from the just-named value factors. There are also other things to consider. One of them is the oil demand, which is very important for the oil shipping industry. Since the oil price is a function of demand, it makes sense to analyze the current trend. Figure 4 shows the daily performance chart of oil futures. As can be seen, it is currently in a downtrend, trading below its 200-day and 50-day simple moving averages. While this looks bearish at first, oil demand is still expected to rise this year.

Apart from that, new shipping routes that might be opened up due to climate change could allow for faster and, thereby, less expensive shipping costs, increasing the margins of the industry.

Technicals

From a technical perspective, DHT looks interesting. As can be seen in Figure 5, the stock trades above both moving averages. While 1-year momentum is negative, it is still far better than most competitors like New Fortress Energy (NASDAQ: NFE), as can be seen in Figure 6. Furthermore, at a positive 6-month momentum of 5.92%, the company is far better than the industry median of -10% to -15% on both time frames. All of this indicates further upside potential for the company in the short term.

Conclusion

In total, DHT looks like one of the best oil shipping companies investors can find at the moment, with almost no weakness compared to its competitors. At the same time, the company trades at an attractive valuation and seems interesting for dividend investors at a dividend yield of more than 8%. The same also goes for the technical valuation, which indicates positive short-term momentum. Because of this, DHT is a buy for me.

data.title: Invesco International Small-Mid Company Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Standardized performance (%) as of March 31, 2025

Performance quoted is past performance and cannot guarantee comparable future results; current performance may be lower or higher. Visit invesco.com for the most recent month-end performance. Performance figures reflect reinvested distributions and changes in net asset value (NAV). Investment return and principal value will vary so that you may have a gain or a loss when you sell shares. Returns less than one year are cumulative; all others are annualized. As the result of a reorganization on May 24, 2019, the returns of the fund for periods on or prior to May 24, 2019 reflect performance of the Oppenheimer predecessor fund. Share class returns will differ from the predecessor fund due to a change in expenses and sales charges. Index sources: Invesco, RIMES Technologies Corp. Please keep in mind that high, double-digit returns are highly unusual and cannot be sustained. Had fees not been waived and/or expenses reimbursed in the past, returns would have been lower. Performance shown at NAV does not include the applicable front-end sales charge, which would have reduced the performance.

Class Y and R6 shares have no sales charge; therefore performance is at NAV. Class Y shares are available only to certain investors. Class R6 shares are closed to most investors. Please see the prospectus for more details.

Manager perspective and outlook

  • During the quarter, apparent uncertainty about US industrial, tax and trade policy appeared to dampen equity results, particularly in the US. International equities outperformed.
  • Small and mid-sized companies performed worse than their larger peers, particularly in the US. In general, we expect proposed US tariff taxes and the recession they may cause to be a greater threat to smaller companies than larger ones.
  • As of two days after the announcement of US import tariffs on April 2nd, all equity markets had declined. International equities continued to outperform, especially in the small- and mid-cap segment. As we have often noted, quality measures for US smid-cap companies - such as debt levels – are poor compared to international smid-caps. Therefore, operating through business slowdowns is expected to be more challenging for them.
  • Growth rate forecasts have naturally been lowered and recession is in our view a significant possibility, as is inflation from higher prices on imported and domestically produced goods. Earnings of the fund’s companies are driven by structural growth trends. In our experience, positive secular trends have grown faster than the average economy during recessions. Also, we choose companies for their pricing power, which supports margins during inflationary periods. Finally, with strong balance sheets and little to no debt, they may better operate through painful economic environments.

As of 03/31/25. Holdings are subject to change and are not buy/sell recommendations.

Portfolio positioning

Our high conviction, low turnover portfolio has a consistent structure that changes little from quarter to quarter. We are benchmark independent, bottom-up investors, focusing on company fundamentals rather than using a “top-down” perspective to overweight or underweight any sector or region. We seek companies we believe to be high quality growth compounders. They have been consistently profitable; have exhibited high internal rates of return on their capital; have funded their operations and growth via internally generated cash flow without bank financing; and historically have strong balance sheets.

We added five new positions during the quarter, three of which we can discuss.

ANDRITZ (OTCPK:ADRZF)(OTCPK:ADRZY), an Austrian company, is a niche provider of equipment and services for hydropower stations. The company in our view has strong growth prospects to increase already robust profitability and cash flow generation. In our opinion, ANDRITZ’s current valuation is very attractive.

Elisa (OTCPK:ELMUF)(OTCPK:ELMUY) is in our view a reliable and cash generative provider of telecommunications services in Finland, a difficult market to navigate with limited threat of new competition. Elisa historically produces internal rates of return almost 2x higher than the average European competitor.

PT Telkom Indonesia is a telecommunications service provider with state ownership, one of Indonesia’s two largest providers and the most profitable. It has strong market share in rural areas that are harder to penetrate. We believe the company is well positioned to benefit from Indonesia’s strong growth in gaming and household broadband access.

We exited 12 positions during the quarter.

Bravida, a Swedish technical service and installation company, has a diversified client base and focuses on small projects. We sold the position due to deteriorating internal rates of return and decreased confidence in company governance.

Britannia Industries is an India-based company that manufactures food products. The company is a market share leader in India’s cookie market. We maintain a favorable view of the company; however, valuation rose to levels we found unattractive.

Descartes Systems (DSGX), a Canadian company that produces supply chain management software, has performed well for the fund and we maintain a favorable view of the business. However, a rising valuation made the risk/reward profile in our view less attractive at this time.

We also sold LEM, NICE (NICE)(OTCPK:NCSYF), Tinexta, Vaisala (OTCPK:VAIAF), Forbo (OTCPK:FBOHF)(OTCPK:FBOHY), Interoll, Medistim, Restore, Zuken and Technoprobe.

Performance highlights

The fund outperformed most in the information technology, health care and communication services sectors, all due to stock selection.

The fund underperformed most in financials, due to the typical underweight allocation, and in the materials and industrials sectors due to stock selection.

Contributors to performance

Carl Zeiss, based in Germany, has been a key fund holding since 2011. The name Zeiss is synonymous with lenses, and the company is a global leader in ophthalmic and other high-precision surgery. Management announced earnings above consensus forecasts.

VZ in Switzerland provides investment management services. Its competitive position improved with the removal of Credit Suisse. Earnings were above consensus forecasts.

TOTVS in Brazil supplies Portuguese language software to corporate clients, dominating the market. Earnings were above consensus forecasts.

Knorr-Bremse, based in Germany, is the world’s leading supplier of braking systems for railroads (50% market share compared to 15% share for the next biggest competitor) and commercial vehicles (40% market share vs. 34% for the nearest competitor).

Aftermarket maintenance contracts can have a 30- to 40-year tenure. Knorr announced earnings above consensus forecasts.

CTS Eventim is a German company similar to Ticketmaster in the US and is similarly dominant in Europe for theater, concerts and sporting events. The company announced 2024 revenue growth of 19%.

Detractors from performance

Greggs in the UK runs a quick-serve, fresh-food network of 2,200 outlets. Greggs announced sales growth below consensus forecasts and the share price fell from its record high.

Biotage is a Swedish maker of high-tech separation and purification equipment used in life sciences and varied industrial settings.

The share price has been relatively volatile after the company’s merger with Astrea Bioseparations.

SMS in Japan operates a website for elders and their caregivers. Despite the company’s first mover advantage, new competitors have entered the market, seemingly raising investor concerns about profitability.

NICE produces software for optimizing call center operations and customer service. It has been transitioning to a cloud subscription model. Management announced slower-than- expected growth of cloud revenue.

DISCO (OTCPK:DSCSY)(OTCPK:DISPF), based in Japan, is a leading global provider of cutting and grinding machinery for the semiconductor industry. Visibility on global semiconductor demand trends has declined.

Invesco International Small-Mid Company Fund Q1 2025 by Invesco US

data.title: Can The Profit Train Keep Rolling For Wabtec?
data.description:
On April 23, Westinghouse Air Brake Technologies (NYSE: WAB), commonly known as Wabtec, announced Q1 2025 earnings and provided updated guidance for the remainder of its fiscal year. Earnings of $2.28 per share were 12.3% better than analyst expectations and 20.6% better than the same number from a year ago. Revenues of $2.6 billion were about 4.5% better than last year and on par with previous forecasts. Wabtec also increased its own projections for profits for the remainder of 2025.

The stock responded immediately with a double-digit gain and now stands at a price point not seen since February. By most metrics, Wabtec appears to be clicking on all cylinders, but I believe WAB’s current stock price reflects the company’s recent good news. Furthermore, it appears the stock price also reveals that the market may expect there will be more positive surprises ahead. I currently rate the stock as a hold based on an analysis of not only the company’s financial reports but also the larger macro picture facing its industry in the coming year.

Company Overview

With a market value of almost $33 billion, Westinghouse Air Break Technologies has been a publicly traded company since 1995. The history of Wabtec goes back over 100 years before the company went public, when the company was founded by George Westinghouse. Westinghouse’s invention of the air brake in 1872 transformed the rail industry, making transportation safer and more efficient.

Fast forward to today, and the company refers to itself as “the world’s foremost rail technology company”. Wabtec is in the early stages of a new strategic plan known as “Freight 2030”. Included in its goals are efforts to reduce dependence on fossil fuels, enhance freight safety, and develop technology to increase rail utilization with existing infrastructure. According to the Pennsylvania Chamber of Business and Industry, the Freight 2030 plan could wipe out 120 tons of emissions per year with “improvements in freight rail utilization and developments in zero-emission locomotive technology”.

The company divides its modern-day operations into two divisions – freight and transit. Together, these divisions generated $10.4 billion in sales in 2024. The company made $7.56 per share in adjusted earnings last year, an increase of almost 28% from 2023. The profit increase was attributed to both rising revenues and improved margins. As shown in the slide below from the company’s Q4 2024 earnings presentation, Wabtec expects more impressive growth over the next five years, increasing revenues by 4 to 6% on an annual basis and improving margins by at least 3.5%.

As part of that final 2024 earnings report, the company announced plans to divest itself of multiple low-margin product lines that represent about $100 million in forgone revenues. The company will take a charge of about $40 million to write down non-cash assets associated with these products.

Analysis of Wabtec

Based on the company’s most recent quarterly report, there is a lot of good news to share about WAB, and this good news seems to permeate both business divisions. In the Freight division, sales were up 4.2%. Adjusted operating margins improved 160 basis points. In the Transit division, sales improved 5.3% while adjusted margins rose 190 basis points. The company also boasts a backlog of orders that represents almost two full years of sales. The 12-month backlog at the end of March was almost $8.2 billion, while the total backlog was just over $22 billion, up 2.1% from the previous quarter. This kind of visibility certainly gives support for optimism in the remainder of this year and beyond.

Analysts are currently expecting earnings of 8.69 for 2025 and 9.72 for 2026. If these projections prove true, that would represent an improvement of 15% for this year and 12% for the following year. While the company has a PE ratio that is a bit higher than its peers, this kind of earnings growth would seem to justify a higher valuation. However, the projections for 2025 are slightly higher than the midpoint for the company's own improved expectations. It does appear that the stock is priced for a best case scenario.

While the company does not pay a large dividend, WAB has been returning profits to shareholders through its stock repurchase plan that recaptured $98 million in shares over the last three months. The company has reduced its overall share count by 5.5% over the last 24 months. The dividend of $1.00 that is to be paid this year is a 25% increase from last year's total payout of $0.80, but the yield is still only 0.52%.

While not included in Wabtec's financial reporting, there is one other bright spot to highlight for the company. WAB was recently recognized by Goldman Sachs for its growing return on equity. The company was listed as one of 50 stocks from the S&P 500 with the highest expected ROE growth over the next 12 months.

Analysis of the Industry

There are conflicting reports of just where the rail freight industry is headed over the next five years. Wabtec appears cautiously optimistic about its ability to grow both its top and bottom lines moving forward, and there are some independent sources to back that up. For example, London-based market research provider Technavio has predicted that rail transportation in North America will grow 7.3% annually for the next five years, thanks to efficiencies created through the use of artificial intelligence.

However, a recent report from the Association of American Railroads, or AAR, shows that there may be real headwinds building. One of the largest potential roadblocks to Wabtec’s projections is the current uncertainty around international trade and tariffs. Global trade accounts for almost 40% of rail carloads and intermodal units and an additional 37% of total rail revenue in the US. A slowdown in trade would cause a decrease in demand for commercial transportation.

As the graph below shows, total carloads have fallen in the US for five of the last six months and 12 of the last 14 months. Out of the 20 categories of freight that are tracked by the AAR, 14 have declined in volume over the last year. Coal is the worst performing of those categories with declines for 14 straight months.

The are counterpoints to these trends that would work in Wabtec’s favor. First, Wabtec is diversified well beyond the US market. Its largest reported increase in rail freight volume for the last quarter was in Kazakhstan, with growth of 14.9%. The European Environment Agency forecasts that rail activity in Europe will grow by over 26% in the next five years. This estimate is deemed as a conservative one, as national statistics for rail freight are not always recorded consistently by all countries.

Growth is also projected to take place at about the same pace in South America, another key area for WAB. Investments in rail infrastructure are expected to be significant in countries like Brazil, Chile, and Argentina. International growth is a big part of Wabtec's own internal projections for growth as shown in the slide below from the company's Q1 2025 Earnings Presentation.

Risks to Consider for WAB

WAB is at the mercy of broad macroeconomic factors that fall outside of the company’s control. Declining demand for rail freight transportation, decreases in capital expenditures, and even geopolitical conflict are all contact threats to the company’s profitability. Investments in emerging markets like Kazakhstan have the potential for great gains but also carry a significant amount of risk. WAB’s sales growth is heavily dependent on economies outside of the US. The effect that any new tariffs may have on global trade has yet to be fully understood.

While other industrial stocks are linked to a move diverse line of products and services, Wabtec is almost completely dependent on rail transportation, making it more vulnerable to threats to that one sector of the industry. For example, as trains become more dependent on technology, specifically AI and specialized software, they become more vulnerable to disruptions caused by network and cyber threats.

It is important to note that Wabtec also operates in a competitive business environment. Companies like Siemens (OTCPK: SIEGY) and Alstom SA (OYCPK: ALSMY) are two of the larger threats to WAB's market share. Changes in pricing or new products from those companies could cause margin contraction for Wabtec.

The Bottom Line

I think there is a lot to like about Wabtec, and if this stock were trading at a slightly lower valuation, I would be more optimistic. In the end, it appeared to me that the stock is currently priced as if the market is paying much more attention to the positives for the company and ignoring most of the potential threats. WAB is currently trading near the top of its five year average in PE and at the top in Price/ Sales and Price/ Cash Flow metrics. When looking at the industry as a whole, I think the risks and the potential rewards are fairly balanced here.

Seeking Alpha’s Quant Rating for WAB seems to match up with my analysis. The company seems to be firing on all cylinders, but the valuation has become a bit stretched thanks to a bounce of almost 19% since its lows last month.

I think the company is a decent hold for those who have already invested, but I would not be adding to any position unless we get the benefit of another market pullback.

data.title: Is Your Strategy Too Narrow For This Market?
data.description:
By Mike Larson

Mike: Are you the kind of investor who focuses mostly on one thing or everything? Well, my next guest is firmly in the latter camp: Fundamentals, technicals, economics, monetary and fiscal policy, and stocks - naturally. But also bonds, currencies, commodities, and more.

If you see the work he and his team put out, you know how comprehensive it is. Now it's time to learn from him what he's seeing in this market and what you should be looking for next. His name is Paul Hickey, and he's co-founder of Bespoke Investment Group. Paul, thanks for joining me today.

So Paul, glad you could join me here. I know I haven't had you on the podcast before, so why don't you talk just a little bit about your background and what it is that you do there at Bespoke.

Paul: Yeah, so I mean, I think what we do at Bespoke - the best way to describe it - is we do a fusion approach to the market, where we look at historicals, we look at quantitative aspects of the market, we look at fundamentals, and we look at technicals. And we put that all together, based on the premise that following solely one discipline toward the market and applying it can lead to errors. But if you look at it from multiple angles and you get agreement across these different angles and disciplines, it can improve your odds of success.

What I learned in my first job out of college - working on an emerging markets trading desk in the late '90s, when Thailand’s currency was imploding and emerging markets were in flux, the US stock market was doing great. But then about four or five weeks later, the news started to leak out that Thailand was falling apart. Not necessarily leak out, but people started paying more attention to it more, and that caused it.

So, we feel like as long as we can look at the market - our primary focus is the equity market - but recognizing that you have to be aware of every aspect of financial markets, geopolitical issues, because they all come back and find their way, price into equities.

Mike: Yeah, I’ll tell you - I look at the research you put out: It’s technicals, fundamentals, overseas, domestic, asset classes, market timing - it’s kind of the whole gamut. So how do you keep track of it all, and is there anything in this market that you think is particularly important for investors to focus on?

Paul: Yeah, so I mean, this is obviously - saying it’s an interesting time would be an understatement. We’ve seen record volatility in April; it’s one of the most volatile months in market history, and that’s really caused some big moves.

But just stepping back a bit - coming into this year, we came off two great years in the market, back-to-back 20% gains. We saw over 50 new highs last year in the S&P 500. On a closing basis, the S&P 500 never closed below its 200-day moving average. All of those are great in the moment, but eventually, those types of environments don’t last forever.

Coming into this year, we didn’t expect the type of volatility we’re seeing now and the type of declines we saw in April. But we were cautioning that gains would be harder to come by because, following prior years we saw that many new highs, like an average of once a week, or we saw the S&P never closing below its 200-day average, forward returns in the next year tended to be weaker than average.

So, it was a little bit of caution. No one expected what happened this year, especially on Liberation Day on April 2nd. So that was a big surprise to markets and investors. The market reacted correctly and swiftly and gave a big smack in the face to the policy of the administration, those, what were called at the time "reciprocal tariffs" - and we're being nice.

Mike: Yeah, it’s funny - I came into this year kind of expecting more volatility, just the way the Trump administration does policy, handles that - not from a political stance, just the surprises all. It’s not the traditional way that Washington does things. It's interesting - I didn’t expect volatility like this. So I'm kind of curious - I think you had a recent TV appearance where you suggested that maybe we may have seen the worst of this post-trade tariff sell-off. I'm curious if you can talk a little more about that. Do you still think that’s the case? And if so, why?

Paul: Yeah, I think so. Again, so this is somewhat of a unique period. We could wake up tomorrow and see tariffs set at 100% for the world - and that would obviously cause markets to fall apart quickly. We’re in this strange moment where just one Truth Social post or tweet can cause problems. So that's something to keep in mind. But looking at the market, people always say, “This time is different,” but in reality the market has been there, done that, and has seen these types of moves - what we think is unique - has seen things similar in the past. So what we’re always doing is listening to the messages of the market.

And getting back to your original question about volatility - we saw a record spike in the VIX, and we saw almost a record decline in the VIX over such short periods of time. Historically, that has usually meant - when you see that decline in the VIX, the worst was over and market returns were positive going forward. Just last week, we saw a back to back to back, a three-peat of 1.5% [daily] gains in the S&P 500 - that's very uncommon. But when you look back historically, about 10 other times - I don't remember the exact number of occurrences - but following those kind of gains, over the next year the S&P was positive again everytime, and it was an average return and a median return of over 20%. So, again, that's another item.

And the lastly, we also saw the Nasdaq 100 breadth was - every stock in the Nasdaq 100 was up one day last week. And that's the type of situation where you see this rushing to buy into the market. It's usually been a sign of gains that are enduring and a rally that will have some endurance to it rather than just a flash in the pan.

Mike: Yeah, well, the Kansas City Chiefs couldn’t get the three-peat done, but it’s good to see the S&P did, right?

Paul: Exactly.

Mike: Let’s talk a little bit about economics, Fed policy and so on. I mean, I’ve seen a lot of headlines thrown out, mostly - you called out one about the Dallas Fed being the worst since COVID. Or this indicator is the worst since GFC, and so on and so forth. Is it just because things were such a shock, or do you think we are going to really have a hard data econonic falloff from what's being going on here?

Paul: Well, so this is what the really fascinating part is about it. You hear this discussion, like you just mentioned... we spoke about the Dallas Fed - the survey-based soft economic data, which has been very weak for months now. And what we've been waiting for is for the soft data to make its way into the hard data - and we haven't quite seen that yet. And so, that’s the divide that most people are expecting to converge. Quite frankly, we would expect some convergence there, but you never know - no one knows the future.

So, what we’re closely monitoring is, right now especially, is jobless claims - which we see every week, and surprisingly they've been pretty firm, we haven't seen a big uptick in the jobless claims. And secondly, with earnings season, we're looking for earnings results. With each earnings report that comes in, we're getting another breadcrumb as to what is going on. It sort of reminds me of back during COVID, when we would watch the case counts updates everyday - and if it was a big uptick in cases the market would sell off, if it was a low number the market would rally. So, when you see these positive earnings reports, the market will rally on it, whereas these negative reports will cause people to be a little bit more downbeat.

So, the results have been surprisingly strong and, obviously, make the argument that that's backward-looking from Q1. What has been surprising to us is, while you've seen a lot of companies pull back guidance, we've seen an equal percentage of companies raise guidance versus lowered guidance. And, I don't know about you, but I wasn't expecting more than a handful of companies to raise guidance this earnings season. What would be the incentive to do so given all the uncertainty out there. So that's something that we can be a little bit optimistic about given what's going on.

But again, the lagged effects of the tariff policy, we're going to start to see that going forward. And if we do see a resolution, if we start to see deals in the coming days, I think the worst of the market declines will certainly be behind us. Because even though we've erased most of April's declines, we were still down about 10% heading into the month.

Mike: That actually leads to the next question. I was going to briefly touch on technicals. You noted something the other day about XLK, the tech sector, was up 11% in five days but was still down 10% YTD. When you look at the technical picture and the health or lack of health in this market, what do you see out there?

Paul: Yeah, I think we’re stuck in a little bit of a no man’s land - between the trenches of the positives and the negatives. We’ve broken some downtrends from the February highs, which is something to be encouraged about, but we’re still below some resistance levels, still below the the 50- and 200-day moving averages.

So we’ve sort of stalled in the last couple of days in the market - not necessarily stalled out, but the S&P as of Tuesday was up six days in a row. So, I think in that resepect it's - the momentum's lowering, but that just has investors questioning, "Oh, have we seen the best?" Because when you've seen the sharp declines of April, you're still a little shell-shocked, so you're on the lookout for anything that can go wrong.

Mike: Alright. Any thoughts on other parts of the market? I mean, obviously gold's been a big story for some people, hitting record highs and having that kind of big reversal and pullback here. Interest rates are in a lot of people's conversations. Any thoughts on either of those kinds of assets as well?

Paul: Yeah, so I think it’s been interesting. Because in the middle of the month we saw equities selling off, and we saw interest rates not going down. And that caused a lot of concerns over Brand America and the attractiveness of US assets.

Our view at the time was that you couldn't base a big trend like that based on two weeks of trading. There were hedge funds positioning that was causing all sorts of flux in the markets. So when you see record volatility in equities, 10% declines over the course of a couple of days, that's going to mess things all up - it’s like throwing a boulder into a pond. You started to see a normalization of the equity market and bonds for the month.

What stands out, though, is the dollar. The dollar has not recovered this month. And for the last two months, it's down close to 8% - which is the type of decline that is, depending on where it finishes the month, it could be the worst two-month drop since December 1987. We haven't seen that kind of move in the dollar in a long time. And in some ways the administration is getting just what it wanted - it wanted a weaker dollar. So that's something to watch. As far as how it impacts the equity market - we were just looking at it this morning - it hasn't really negatively impacted stocks. Forward equity returns of the equity market have actually been slightly above the average, long-term average, when you've seen a 7.5% decline over two months.

Mike: As we get to the tail end of our conversation, any key takeaways? Things that you'd want to highlight - the most important thing or two that somebody could take away here. I mean, any kind of sectors you really like, sectors you don't, stocks you like or don't? Things like that, I'm wondering what all you might be able to offer up.

Paul: Yeah, I think two things to keep in mind. First, as far as the stock basis goes, especially in the case of individual investors looking for the biggest winners - if you look 20 years from now, the biggest winners are not going to be - who know they could be! - but I would bet you just about any amoutn of money that they're not going to be the Mag 7 stocks. Those stocks have seen their gains, they've seen explosive growth. That's not to say they won't do well or they neccessarily have to go down, but the bigger winners are going to be found in other areas where everyone's not focusing. You go back to the 2000 peak - of those stocks, the only one that's really in the top now as it was then is Microsoft (MSFT). All the other top 10 stocks then have underperformed the market by a wide margin. So, you want to broaden your horizons and not just focus on what you're seeing in the papers or on TV everyday.

And the other thing to keep in mind too is, with the biggest winners of any period, and especially for the market too, you want to tune out some of the noise. If I told you the stocks that were going to be the big winners 20 years from now, you still may not have the stomach to withstand the volatility. Like, a stock like Nvidia (NVDA), over the last 20 years, saw at least three drawdowns of over 50%, one drawdown of 90%. For any investor to hold it through that entire period would have been a really difficult endeavor if they were following the market on a day-to-day basis.

Mike: Thanks, Paul - appreciate your time.

Paul: Thanks, Mike. Thanks, everyone.

Originally published on MoneyShow.com
